coredata.dc:title: Green and facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for multicolor cellular imaging and Co2+ sensing in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Xiangping
Shi, Lihong
Wen, Guangming
Li, Yanyan
Dong, Chuan
Yang, Jun
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: We report a facile and eco-friendly strategy for the fabrication of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) and demonstrate their applications for multicolor cellular imaging and Co2+ sensing in living cells. One-pot hydrothermal method using pigskin as the precursor yields bright green-emitting N-CDs, providing an easy way for the production of N-CDs without a tedious synthetic methodology or the toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Doping N heteroatoms benefits the generation of N-CDs with stronger fluorescence emission. As-prepared N-CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including high fluorescent quantum yield (24.1%), excellent biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and satisfactory chemical stability. Depending on these superior properties, as-synthesized N-CDs have been successfully used for multicolor cellular imaging. More strikingly, as-synthesized N-CDs generates strong response to Co2+ ions and gives rise to the fluorescence quenching and further application is extended to precise and quantitative detection of Co2+ ions in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Nitrogen-doped
Multicolor cellular imaging
Co2+ sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475080 , 21575084 , 21575083 and 21306108 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 2015011020 , 2015112 and 2013011009-5 ), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China , Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of N-CDs from hydrothermal treatment of pigskin, along with photographs of the corresponding samples under daylight and 460nm excitation.
(a) TEM image of N-CDs. (b) The corresponding size distribution histogram of N-CDs.
(a) Raman spectra of N-CDs. (b) XPS spectrum of N-CDs. (c) C1s, (d) O1s and (e) N1s spectra of N-CDs. (f) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.
(a) Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. Inset: photographs of the corresponding samples under sunlight and 460nm excitation. (b) Excitation dependent emission spectra of N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence decay of N-CDs at 521nm (excitation at 460nm). (b) Effect of pH on the PL intensity of N-CDs. (c) Dependence of PL intensity at 521nm on excitation time for N-CDs under 365nm excitation. (d) Effect of storage time on the PL intensity at 521nm of N-CDs.

(a) Effect of different metal ions on F/F0 of N-CDs solution (0.35mg/mL) at 521nm. (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of Co2+ (1.0×10−6 M-3.0×10−4 M). (c) plot of (F0-F)/F versus Co2+ concentrations. Inset: the good linearity in the range of 1–100μM.
Viability (%) of Hela cells after 24h treatment with N-CDs calculated from MTT assay.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy image of Hela cells labelled with the N-CDs (0.35mg/mL) under 405nm excitation (b), 488nm excitation (c), and 547nm excitation (d). (a) bright field. (e) The merged image of a and b. (f) The merged image of a and c. (g) The merged image of a and d. (h) The merged image of b, c, and d.

Dynamic fluorescence imaging of Hela cells. (a–g) Image in green channel; (h) bright-field image of panel a; (i) temporal profile of average fluorescence intensity in regions of interest 1 shown in panel a–g. Excitation wavelength was 488nm, and emission was collected in the green channel (425–475nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Green and facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for multicolor cellular imaging and Co2+ sensing in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Western Ontario, Department of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Materials Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a facile and eco-friendly strategy for the fabrication of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) and demonstrate their applications for multicolor cellular imaging and Co2+ sensing in living cells. One-pot hydrothermal method using pigskin as the precursor yields bright green-emitting N-CDs, providing an easy way for the production of N-CDs without a tedious synthetic methodology or the toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Doping N heteroatoms benefits the generation of N-CDs with stronger fluorescence emission. As-prepared N-CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including high fluorescent quantum yield (24.1%), excellent biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and satisfactory chemical stability. Depending on these superior properties, as-synthesized N-CDs have been successfully used for multicolor cellular imaging. More strikingly, as-synthesized N-CDs generates strong response to Co2+ ions and gives rise to the fluorescence quenching and further application is extended to precise and quantitative detection of Co2+ ions in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials have generated increasing attention due to their abundant optical properties and their myriad applications in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, and optoelectronic devices [1–3]. Carbon nanomaterials mainly included nanodiamonds [4], carbon nanotubes [5], fullerene [6], and graphene [7–10], carbon nanodots (CDs) [11]. Among them, fluorescent CDs, emerging as a rising star in the carbon nanomaterial family, have been magnificently transformed into nanolights in many applications [12–14]. By virtue of good biocompatibility, low toxicity, chemical inertness, and ease of preparation, CDs are environmentally benign alternatives to quantum dots comprised of heavy metals [12,15,16].

To data, tremendous efforts have been made for developing synthesis methods for various types of CDs. These approaches can be classified into two main groups: top-down [17–19] and bottom-up methods [20–22]. However, these methods have some limitations in terms of large-scale production because they usually involve harsh synthesis conditions, complex processes, and low quantum yields (QY) (always less than 10%). Considering that QY is of great importance for their practical applications, a facile method for fabricating CDs with distinguished satisfactory QY is a challenging but worthy issue.

Doping CDs with heteroatoms can effectively tune their intrinsic properties, including electronic properties, surface and local chemical reactivities [23,24]. The N atom, having a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms, has been widely used for chemical doping of CDs [25–28]. In view of the remarkable quantum-confinement and edge effects, doping CDs with electron-rich N atoms could effectively increase the QY of CDs. Zhang et al. [29] proposed an easy strategy to achieve N-CDs with QY of approximately 22% by one-pot solvothermal route using CCl4 and NaNH2 as starting materials. Zhao et al. [30] reported an economical synthetic method for producing highly luminescent N-CDs by microwave treatment of ionic liquids with QY of approximately 25.8%. Sun et al. [31] developed a facile and cost-effective approach for the synthesis of N-CDs with sulfuric acid carbonization and etching of hair fiber with QY of approximately 11.1%. However, these synthesis methods have raised concerns over environmental harm, and high cost. The green and economical synthetic strategy of high-quality N-CDs is still imminently desirable toward the use of nontoxic chemicals, environmentally friendly solvents, and renewable materials [32].

Nature provides a limitless source, which gives material scientists the freedom to have inspiration for new ideas, new ways of developing nanomaterials with novel structures and properties, and less environmental impact. Pigskin is an inexpensive, easily available material. In this work, we attempt to prepared highly fluorescent N-CDs from pigskin by one-step hydrothermal treatment. The process is environmentally friendly, simple, and efficient. Due to their favorable properties, such as high fluorescent QY, low-toxicity, and satisfactory stability, as-prepared N-CDs have been applied for multicolor cellular imaging and Co2+ sensing in living cells.

AlCl3, BaCl2, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2, BiCl3, HgCl2, KCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, NiCl2, and ZnCl2 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. (Beijing, China). NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pigskin was purchased from local market (Taiyuan, China). Rhodamin 6G was obtained from E. Merck, Darmstadt. 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity higher than 18MΩcm−1 (Bedford, MA, USA). All the reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

N-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of pigskin. Typically, pigskin was washed with water and then dried in air prior to use. Pigskin (0.5g) was dissolved in DDI water (20mL) at room temperature. The cloudy solution was poured into a 50mL high pressure reactor vessel, placed in an oven, and then heated at 250°C for 2h. After cooling down to room temperature, N-CDs were collected by removing larger particles through centrifugation at 4000rpm for 10min and then dialysing for 2days.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was carried out in a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples for TEM measurements were prepared by placing a drop of colloidal solution on carbon-coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. The size distribution of N-CDs was performed by counting over 100 particles. Fluorescent photographs of N-CDs under UV light of 365nm were operated with ZF-2 ultraviolet analyzer for three purposes from Shanghai City Anting electronic instrument factory. Fluorescence photographs of G-CDs under 470nm excitation were taken using a multispectral fluorescence in vivo molecular imaging system (S-0010A). Raman spectroscopy was characterized using Bruker Senterra dispersive Raman microscopy with laser wavelengths at 532nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an AXIS ULTRA DLD electron spectrometer from Shimadzu Company using 300W Al Kα radiation. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Nicolet Magna 550 spectrometer using a resolution of 4cm−1. The sample with 1mg diluted by KBr (ratio 1:200) was pressed into the disc. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (China). Steady-state fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluo-rometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). A nanosecond hydrogen flash lamp was used as the excitation light source in the determination of fluorescence lifetime.

The relative fluorescence QY (Φ) of the N-CDs was calculated using the equation: Φ x = Φ std I x A std η 2 x/(I std A x η 2 std). The optical densities were measured using a Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer. In the equation, I x and I std are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs and the standard, respectively. A x and A std denote the optical densities (OD) of the N-CDs and the standard, respectively. Rhodamin 6G in ethanol was chosen as a standard with a QY of 0.94 at 488nm η x and η std denote the refractive indices of the N-CDs and the standard, respectively. The absorbances of all the samples in a 1.0cm cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength to minimize re-absorption effects.

The detection of Co2+ was performed at room temperature in 10mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (containing 150mM NaCl, pH 7.0). In a typical run, 10μL of N-CDs dispersion (1.4mg/mL) was added into 300μL of PBS, followed by the addition of Co2+ standard with various concentrations. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1min at room temperature.

For the cell cytotoxicity text, Hela cells were first plated on a Costar 96-well tissue-culture cluster and cultured at 37°C with 5% CO2 in air for 3h to adhere cells onto the surface. The well without cells and treatment with N-CDs was taken as a zero set. The medium was then changed with 100μL of fresh DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS containing N-CDs, and the cells were allowed to grow for another 24h. At least five parallel samples were performed in each group. Cells without treatment with N-CDs were taken as a control. After adding 20μL of 5.0mg/mL MTT reagent into individual well, the cells were further incubated for 4h, followed by removing the culture medium with MTT, and then 150μL of DMSO was added. The resulting mixture was shaken for ca. 10min at room temperature. The OD of the mixture was measured at 490nm with a SunRisemicroplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, Grödig, Austria). The cell viability was estimated using the equation of Cell Viability (%) = (OD Treated/OD Control)×100%, where OD Control and OD Treated were obtained in the absence and presence of N-CDs, respectively.

Hela cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and incubated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The N-CDs aqueous solution (500μL, 1.4mg/mL) was added to the culture medium (2.0mL) at 0.35mg/mL final concentration. After incubation for 0.5h, Hele cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin/0.020% EDTA, washed three times (1.0mL each) with PBS (comprising 137mM NaCl, 2.7mM KCl, 8.0mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0mM KH2PO4). After that, the cells were treated with Co2+ (100μM) in 2mL PBS (pH 7.0) for additional 1min. Fluorescence images were collected with an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser-scanning microscope.

The successful fabrication of N-CDs was carried out by using pigskin as the starting materials via a one-step hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). Pigskin mainly consists of proteins, which may serve as widespread, easily available, low-cost, and green natural carbon sources for large-scale fabrication of N-CDs. The as-prepared N-CDs freely disperse in water and appear transparent. The pH value of these fresh N-CDs is ca. 5 because of the formation of carboxyl groups.

The size distribution and morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, N-CDs are spherical and well dispersed. Fig. 2b demonstrates that the size distribution of the N-CDs is relatively small and the majority falls within the range from 3.5 to 7.0nm. Based on the statistical analysis of above one hundred particles, the average diameter of the N-CDs is 5.58±0.21nm. Fig. S1 is typical XRD profiles of the N-CDs. The XRD pattern of the N-CDs has a broader peak at 2θ=23.8, revealing an amorphous carbon phase, which is attributed to the introduction of nitrogen-, and oxygen-containing groups.

The Raman spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3 a) exhibits two broad peaks at around 1344 and 1520cm−1, which are attributed to the D (sp3-hybridized) and G (sp2-hybridized) bands, respectively. The D band is associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon. The G band corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite and is related to the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [21]. The intensity ratios of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (I D/I G) is 0.9823 for N-CDs, indicating that they have a structure similar to graphite. The surface functional groups and element states of N-CDs were characterized by XPS. N-CDs are mainly composed of C, O and N elements (the atom ratio of C:O:N is 19.31:6.02:1). As shown in Fig. 3b, N-CDs show three apparent peaks centered at 282.7, 397.2, and 529.3eV which can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into three surface contents, that is, CC (284.8eV), C-N (286.0eV), CO (288.0eV). In the O1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3d), two peaks at 531.2 and 532.5eV are ascribed to CO and COH/COC, respectively. The N1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3e) indicates the presence of CNC (399.7eV) and NH (401.1eV). The XPS results confirm that N-CDs are functioned with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino moieties. We also used FTIR to identify the functional groups of N-CDs. In FTIR spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3f), the peaks at 3389 and 2929cm−1 are attributed to the OH and CH stretching vibrations, the peaks at 3255cm−1 is attributed to the NH stretching vibrations, the peaks at about 1669cm−1 and 1404cm−1 are attributed to the CO and CN stretching vibrations, the peak at 1110cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching vibrations, and the peak at 1302cm−1 is ascribed to the CH bending vibrations. The functional groups of N-CDs identified by the FTIR are in good agreement with XPS. The zeta potential of N-CDs is −33.0mV, due to the existence of OH/–COOH/–NH2 groups on the surface of N-CDs.

The optical properties of the N-CDs are displayed in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, N-CDs exhibit a strong absorption peak at 260nm together with a shoulder around 293nm. The peak at 260nm is ascribed to p–p* transition of the CC bands of localized sp2 clusters in the core, whereas the shoulder is due to n−p* transition of the CO bands of carboxyl groups on the shell of CDs. When excited at 460nm, their PL spectra show the emission peaks at 521nm, thus leading to strong green fluorescent emission (the inset in Fig. 4a). Additionally, N-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 4b). When N-CDs are excited from 370nm to 500nm, the maximum emission peak shifts from 460nm to 535nm, and the fluorescence intensity increases gradually and then decreases. Using Rhodamine 6G as the reference, the fluorescence QY of N-CDs is measured to be 24.1%.

The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of N-CDs was assessed by fluorescent decay curves (Fig. 5 a). The decay curve is fitted using biexponential functions Y(t) based on non-linear least squares analysis in equation: Y(t)= A 1exp(-t/τ 1)+ A 2exp(-t/τ 2), where A 1 and A 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay life-time of τ 1 and τ 2. Further, according to equation: τ average =(A 1 τ 1 2 + A 2 τ 2 2)/(A 1 τ 1 + A 2 τ 2), we calculate the average lifetime (τ average) of N-CDs as 8.89±0.05ns (χ 2 < 1.16), which is comparable to reported values [33]. The stability of the prepared N-CDs under different conditions was investigated. The PL intensities of N-CDs at pH values from 3.0 to 12.0 are measured (Fig. 5b). Interestingly, it is obvious that the PL intensity of N-CDs is pH independent. Additionally, PL intensity of N-CDs remains almost constant even after UV excitation for four hours (Fig. 5c) and storage for three months (Fig. 5d). The results indicate that N-CDs have excellent stability, making them promising for meaningful applications and commercial purpose.

To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system, the fluorescence spectra in the presence of representative metal ions were examined under pH 7.0 condition at 460nm excitation, including Al3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Bi3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+, as shown in Fig. 6 a. It can be seen that a much lower fluorescence is observed for N-CDs upon addition of Co2+ ions while other metal ions show either no or slight change in the PL intensity relative to the free N-CDs. This result reveals that the as-synthesized N-CDs are highly selective toward Co2+ ions over the other metal ions and could be developed as an efficient fluorescence sensor for Co2+ ions. The Co2+ ions sensing capacity of as-prepared N-CDs was investigated in the range of 1.0×10−6 M to 3.0×10−4 M at pH 7.0. With increasing Co2+ ions concentration, as shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreases progressively, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to Co2+ concentration. Fig. 6c presented the PL data versus the concentration of Co2+ ions. The correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.993 over the concentration range of 1.0×10−6 M–1.0×10−4 M, while the plot does not fit a linear equation over the whole concentration range of 1.0×10−6 M–3.0×10−4 M, indicating that both dynamic and static quenching processes occur in this sensor system [34]. The detection limit of Co2+ was 6.8×10−7 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The results show that the sensor is highly sensitive and has widely linear range.

To further explore the mechanism for Co2+ ions sensing, the UV–vis absorption of the Co2+ and PL spectra of the N-CDs were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, Co2+ possess a wide absorption peak at around from 400 to 550nm. Our asprepared N-CDs were chosen as the fluorophore because both the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs showed quite precise overlapping with the absorption band of Co2+. The fluorescence of N-CDs may be quenched dramatically from Co2+ via the Inner filter effect (IFE) [35]. IFE phenomenon of fluorescence usually occurs between the absorber and fluorophore resulting from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by absorbers in the detection system. The effective IFE requires the complementary overlap of the absorber’s absorption band with the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore [36].

Cytotoxicity is an indispensible parameter for future application of N-CDs especially in live cell-labelling. Toxicity experiments of the N-CDs in vitro were evaluated using Hela cell lines through the MTT assay. There exists a dose-dependent cytotoxicity on the Hela cells after the initial 24-h exposure to the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 , the cell viability decreased from 100% to 82% with concentrations of N-CDs from 0.1 to 0.8mg/mL. The results demonstrated the low toxicity of N-CDs to cultured cells under the experimental conditions at the concentration of 0.35mg/mL.

To demonstrate the potential application of N-CDs as a bioimaging probe, in vitro cellular uptake experiments in Hela cells were performed. Hela cells were tagged with the prepared N-CDs and the images were taken under a laser scanning confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. 8 , after being tagged with N-CDs, cells show bright blue PL(Fig. 8b), green PL(Fig. 8c), and red PL(Fig. 8d) when excited with 405nm 488nm, and 547nm laser, respectively. Fig. 8e–g are the merged images with the bright field and the fluorescence images. Thus as-prepared N-CDs can be used as the excellent fluorescence probe for bioimaging.

Co is an essential element required in minute amounts as a component of vitamin B12 for people. Usually, the concentration of cobalt ion in living cells may be very low. However, Cobalt is a toxic metal used in various industrial applications such as batteries, super-alloys, magnetic products and preparation of enamels and pigments. Co is also considered as a possible carcinogen for humans and a toxic element to aquatic organisms [37–39]. Exposure in cobalt environment for long-time or the excessive Co uptake can lead to diseases and death [37]. Hence, it is nessessary to perform cell imaging on Co2+.

We used Hela cells as a model to study Co2+-dependent fluorescence imaging of N-CDs with a confocal fluorescence microscope. Hela cells were incubated with N-CDs (0.35mg/mL) for 0.5h at 37°C, and then with Co2+ (100μM) for 1min. Dynamic fluorescence imaging is performed to observe quenching process in real time by confocal laser scanning microscope. As shown in Fig. 9 , N-CDs with green fluorescence emissions disperse well within living cells. Furthermore, the N-CDs-stained Hela cells exhibit strong green fluorescence (Fig. 9a). After Co2+ ions are added the fluorescence brightness decreases gradually with extended time (Fig. 9b–g). Bright-field image after N-CDs incubation confirmed the viability of the cells (Fig. 9 h). The fluorescence intensity is calculated by commercial software (Fig. 9i). Our results demonstrate that N-CDs can be applied potentially for imaging Co2+ in living cells under a confocal laser scanning microscope.

In summary, we have described an easy, green, and economic route for producing highly fluorescent N-CDs by using ubiquitous and natural pigskin as the precursor via one-step hydrothermal method. As-prepared N-CDs show good fluorescence behavior without further surface treatment. In addition, N-CDs display excellent stability under various conditions, including pH, and continuous excitation. The obtained N-CDs possess good biocompatibility and can be used as excellent fluorescence probes for multicolor cell imaging and detecting Co2+ in living cells. Therefore, we demonstrate herein a facile and eco-friendly strategy to produce highly fluorescent N-CDs from widely available green carbon sources for versatile applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21475080, 21575084, 21575083 and 21306108), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No.2015011020, 2015112 and 2013011009-5), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China, Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.05.066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots for live cells, in vivo imaging, and diagnostics
Selective inhibition of human brain tumor cells through multifunctional quantum-dot-based siRNA delivery
Diamond nanoparticles: jewels for chemistry and physics
A route to brightly fluorescent carbon nanotubes for near-infrared imaging in mice
Synthesis and characterization of a photoluminescent nanoparticle based on fullerene-Silica hybridization
Graphene oxide as a chemically tunable platform for optical applications
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Upconversion and downconversion fluorescent graphene quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation and photocatalysis
Synthesis of large, stable colloidal graphene quantum dots with tunable size
A carbon dots based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of hemoglobin
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Quantum-Sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
Doping graphitic and carbon nanotube structures with boron and nitrogen
Gd(III)-doped carbon dots as a dual fluorescent-MRI probe
One-step synthesis of robust nitrogen-doped carbon dots: acid-evoked fluorescence enhancement and their application in Fe3+ detection
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (II) ions
N-doped carbon dots with high sensitivity and selectivity for hypochlorous acid detection and its application in water
Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging
One-pot synthesis of N-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Ionic liquids as precursors for highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots used for label-free detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ and cell imaging
Hair fiber as a p recursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
One step synthesis of C-dots by microwave mediated caramelization of poly(ethylene glycol)
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for detection of curcumin based on the inner filter effect
Design of fluorescent assays for cyanide and hydrogen peroxide based on the inner filter effect of metal nanoparticles
Health risks associated with cobalt exposure-an overview
Update on the genotoxicity and carcinogenicity of cobalt compounds

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiangping Wen obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Changzhi University in 2013. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in photochemsity.

Lihong Shi is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.

Guangming Wen is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2008. His current research interests include Biosensor and electroanalytical chemistry.
Yanyan Li completed B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Jinzhong University in 2013. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Jun Yang is an associate professor of mechanical and materials engineering at University of Western Ontario. He is the entrants of “Hundred talents program” in Shanxi Province. He has conducted Multiple cademic communication in Institute of Environmental Science of Shanxi University.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.
